explanation: Working stroke angulation for curettes during SRP is 45–90° (ideally ~70° face-to-tooth). Insertion is near 0°; <45° burnishes calculus and fails to cut deposits.
An explorer (e.g., 11/12) is used after SRP to tactilely verify residual calculus and root smoothness. Hoe/chisel remove bulk deposits; mirrors only visualize.

Desquamative gingivitis plus buccal mucosal white striae points to a mucocutaneous blistering/immune disease spectrum (lichen planus most classic for striae; PV/MMP also cause DG). Treat cause, not “desquamative gingivitis” as a final Dx.

Coronally advanced flap with CTG for Miller/RT1 canine recession uses intrasulcular (sulcular) incisions ± vertical releases and periosteal release for passive coronal advance—not resective internal/external bevel gingivectomy cuts.

Overcontoured crowns violating biologic width need provisionalization (hygiene/control inflammation), surgical crown lengthening to reestablish ~2 mm supracrestal attachment, then definitive remakes—never remake finals on inflamed violated tissues first.

Even clinically healthy gingiva shows a sparse resident inflammatory infiltrate (mainly lymphocytes) on histology; “no inflammatory cells” is incorrect. Dense neutrophils mark acute/active disease, not health.

Aplastic anemia with oral lacerations/bleeding reflects thrombocytopenia (low platelets). Neutropenia drives infection/ulcers without clotting failure; low RBCs cause pallor/fatigue, not primary hemorrhage.

Akers clasps on bilateral distal-extension (Kennedy I) premolar abutments transmit unfavorable leverage/torque under function, increasing abutment mobility and overload—design needs stress-breaking/RPI-type consideration.

High palatal vault, crossbite/mouth-breathing pattern, xerostomia, and periodontal risk fit chronic asthma/mouth breathing and inhaler-related dry mouth more than Crohn’s or leukemia as the unifying systemic link.

Marked gingival enlargement with excellent plaque control and daily whitening/flavor toothpaste suggests plasma cell gingivitis (allergic/contact to cinnamon-flavor agents), not plaque-only or classic drug-induced patterns without meds.
Grade I furcation (incipient, probe catches) is managed with OHI, SRP, and odontoplasty/furcationplasty as needed—not GTR, tunneling, or extraction.

Fibrotic post-orthodontic gingival enlargement with adequate attached gingiva is classically reduced by external bevel gingivectomy after inflammation control—not primary internal bevel resective flap unless osseous needs exist.

Mandibular molar grade III through-and-through furcation can be maintained with tunneling when roots diverge and hygiene is excellent; hemisection/root amp sacrifice a root—use when one root is hopeless.

After scaling, lost class V plus new recession on #43 needs restoration of the cervical defect plus soft-tissue graft (CTG/FGG as indicated) if KT/coverage is inadequate—composite alone ignores the soft-tissue deficit.

Among CCB (nifedipine), antiepileptics, and immunosuppressants, nifedipine is the classic hypertension drug linked to drug-induced gingival enlargement; tacrolimus causes less GE than cyclosporine; insulin/metformin do not.
Maxillary first molars have the poorest multi-rooted prognosis in severe periodontitis (three roots, complex furcations, enamel projections, access limits) versus mandibular molars or premolars.

Generalized gingival ulceration, heavy deposits, and systemic fever suggest severe/necrotizing or acute periodontal infection needing professional debridement plus systemic antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin + metronidazole), not CHX alone delayed care.
Marginal ulceration and BOP 1–2 days after SRP are expected soft-tissue trauma/healing—reassure, gentle hygiene, delay re-instrumentation; local AB or immediate re-scaling is not first-line.

Visible implant thread plus PD 4–5 mm implies crestal bone loss with soft-tissue inflammation → peri-implantitis (bone loss required). Mucositis is inflammation without progressive bone loss; recession alone is not peri-implantitis.
Periodontitis is a plaque-biofilm bacterial disease in a susceptible host (dysbiosis of pathobionts); fungi/viruses are not the primary causal agents of chronic periodontitis.
Intraoral surgical extraction with flap, no frank infection/pus, is a clean-contaminated wound (Class II)—oral flora enters controlled surgical field—not clean, contaminated, or dirty/infected.
Class/grade I furcation treatment is nonsurgical: SRP, OHI, and odontoplasty as needed. GTR/tunnel/extraction are for advanced furcation grades with specific anatomy.

Post-ortho fibrotic gingival enlargement after hygiene phase is treated by external bevel gingivectomy when pockets are soft-tissue only—internal bevel/osseous surgery if bone recontouring is required.
Grade I furcation: thorough SRP plus odontoplasty/furcationplasty to improve access and reduce plaque trap; GTR/extraction not indicated for incipient lesions.

Periodontal/gingival abscess with swelling and pus: first-line incision and drainage (and debridement of the pocket), then cause control. RCT only if pulpal involvement; gingivectomy is not acute drainage.
Supportive perio therapy is site-specific: polish/light instrument BOP-only sites; re-scale/root-plane residual deep pockets/CAL sites—not blanket full-mouth heavy SRP or polish-only.
Standard risk assessment cycle: identify hazard → evaluate risk → record findings → review periodically. Informing a supervisor alone is incomplete without documentation and review.
Grade I furcation with otherwise normal probing depths: odontoplasty + SRP/OHI. GTR and root-coverage procedures are not primary therapy for shallow incipient furcations.
Standard periodontal probe tip diameter is approximately 0.5 mm (WHO/CPITN ball tip 0.5 mm; many calibrated probes ~0.4–0.6 mm)—not 1–3 mm tips.

Failed obturation, large restoration, periapical RL continuous to furcation/crest, and mobility indicate a hopeless combined endo-perio/strategic loss—extraction is usually best over root planing or observation alone.

Middle-aged woman, good hygiene, no mucosal lesions, gingival discomfort with dysgeusia/metallic taste fits burning mouth syndrome (diagnosis of exclusion) more than overt leukemia without systemic/oral signs.
Post-SRP marginal ulcers and focal BOP are common short-term healing responses—reassure and support hygiene; do not re-SRP immediately or place local antibiotics as first response.
Short clinical crowns with gingivitis: control plaque/gingivitis with scaling and reassess vertical dimension of gingiva before elective crown lengthening/gingivectomy for esthetics.

Smokers typically do not harbor substantially more plaque quantity than non-smokers; disease severity reflects impaired host response, vasoconstriction, and healing—not simply “more plaque.” Red/orange complex shifts and host imbalance are more accurate.
Easy gingival bleeding on probing corresponds to the established gingivitis lesion dominated by plasma cells (B-cell lineage), after initial neutrophil and early T-lymphocyte phases.

Gracey curettes are area-specific with a unique offset blade (face ~70° to terminal shank) so lower shank parallel yields correct working angle. Universal curettes are 90°; sickles are triangular for supra use.

Interdental CAL 5–6 mm defines Stage III periodontitis (≥5 mm CAL); Grade A is slow progression when risk modifiers/radiographic bone loss:age support slow rate. Stage I/II stop at lower CAL thresholds.
Locally delivered “perio chip” is the chlorhexidine gluconate chip (PerioChip) placed in residual pockets after debridement. Doxycycline is gel/polymer systems; metro is not the classic chip.
Surgically accelerated orthodontics uses selective alveolar decortication/PAOO (not CT graft or occlusal adjustment) to speed tooth movement via regional acceleratory phenomenon.

Osseous resective surgery after nonresponding ~5 mm pockets recontours bone to positive physiologic architecture and reduces pocket depth by apically positioning soft tissue—not primarily to “heal bone” or only treat 3-wall defects (those favor regeneration).
Localized recession without inflammation next to a high/aberrant buccal frenum is frenum-pull related (tension, shallow vestibule), not plaque gingivitis or periodontitis when inflammation is absent.

Vertical root fracture is most reliably confirmed by direct visualization (exploratory surgery/flap, or sometimes after extraction). Probing, bitewings, and PAs can suggest (deep narrow pocket, J-shaped RL) but are not definitive alone.

Desquamative gingivitis plus white striae on buccal mucosa is classic oral lichen planus (erosive form on gingiva). MMP/PV cause DG but typically without Wickham striae; recurrent herpes is not chronic bilateral striae.
Buccally displaced/impacted canines erupt thin buccal bone/soft tissue and commonly present with labial gingival recession and mucogingival problems after exposure/alignment.

Generalized gingivitis despite reported thrice-daily brushing in orthodontics is driven by plaque-retentive brackets/wires/appliances—not primary systemic disease when local plaque control around hardware fails.
ACC/AHA: Stage 1 hypertension is SBP 130–139 or DBP 80–89 mmHg. Readings ~130–139/80 fit Stage 1, not Stage 2 (≥140 or ≥90) and not merely “elevated” (120–129 and <80).

Maxillary first molar is hardest to debride (three roots, MB2 concavities, trifurcation, distal furcation access). Premolars are simpler; second molars are difficult but first molar anatomy is the classic teaching challenge.

False statement: smoking does not reliably increase plaque retention/amount; smokers often have similar plaque scores but worse attachment loss, less BOP (masked), and impaired healing. Smoking as major risk and host–microbe imbalance are true.
2017 Cairo classification: RT1 = gingival recession without interproximal attachment loss (CEJ interproximal not detectable clinically). Miller I is older system; RT2 has interproximal loss ≤ buccal.

Curette insertion angle into the pocket is 0–20° (closed face) to avoid soft-tissue trauma; then open to ~70–80° for the working stroke. 90° gouges root; mid angles without insertion first are incorrect.

Pregnancy gingivitis/periodontitis from plaque is managed with OHI and SRP (preferably second trimester); delaying all care until delivery risks progression and bacteremia—treatment is indicated and safe with precautions.
Williams probe markings are 1-2-3-5-7-8-9-10 mm (notched). UNC-15 is 1–15 mm color-coded; WHO is 0.5 ball with 3.5-5.5-8.5-11.5; Nabers is curved for furcations.

Overhangs retain biofilm and favor shift toward gram-negative anaerobic periodontopathogens and inflammation. Goal of removing overhangs is to reduce pathogenic (gram−) plaque niche—not to promote gram+ growth as primary aim.

Friable, bleeding gingiva with spongiosis and dense plasma cell infiltrate = plasma cell gingivitis (hypersensitivity). Plaque gingivitis is neutrophil/plasma mixed without that hypersensitivity pattern; leukemia needs systemic signs/CBC.
Vertical bitewings best display crestal bone levels and early interproximal bone loss with less distortion than horizontal BWs for perio assessment; PAs alone can foreshorten/elongate crests.
In mixed dentition/young permanent teeth (~6 years), apical PDL fiber groups organize last as roots complete; alveolar crest, horizontal, and oblique groups form earlier along developing root length.

Short root trunk with divergent roots means early furcation entrance and complex multi-surface anatomy—hardest SRP access. Long trunks delay furcation involvement; convergent roots can limit furcation width but short+divergent is especially difficult.
Gingival crevicular fluid volume and inflammatory mediators increase with gingivitis and periodontitis severity; GCF is not absent or reduced in severe disease.

Isolated narrow deep pocket, bite pain, and J-shaped radiolucency indicate vertical root fracture—prognosis is hopeless for the root/tooth; extraction (or root removal) is appropriate, not RCT/SRP as definitive cure.
Peg laterals are a morphodifferentiation (shape/size) disturbance during the bell stage of crown morphogenesis—not initiation (anodontia) or apposition (matrix volume/hypoplasia patterns).

Enamel hypoplasia is defective matrix secretion during the apposition (secretory) stage of amelogenesis. Maturation defects cause hypomineralization with normal thickness; initiation/bud affect tooth number.

Retreatment of distal bone loss on a mandibular first molar with missing second molar uses a distal wedge flap to reduce pocket and thin tuberosity/retromolar tissue—semilunar/submarginal are not the standard distal access design.

For regenerating a 3-wall infrabony defect with adequate interdental space, papilla preservation flaps maximize primary closure and graft containment. Laterally positioned is for recession; apically positioned increases recession for pocket reduction.

Previously treated #26 with sinus tract, percussion/palpation tenderness, isolated deep mesiopalatal pocket, and grade II mobility is highly suggestive of vertical root fracture rather than simple non-VRF post-treatment disease.

Endodontically treated tooth with severe bite pain, isolated deep pocket, and teardrop/J-shaped radiolucency is vertical root fracture until proven otherwise—not mere apical periodontitis or horizontal fracture patterns.

A localized deep periodontal defect on an endodontically treated tooth strongly suggests vertical root fracture (sinus tract, isolated probing). Gingivitis is generalized marginal; resorption has different radiographic contours.

Adjacent natural tooth is vital, painless, grade 3 mobile with short root—manage periodontally (often hopeless extraction for implant site planning) and follow vitality; do not initiate endodontics on a vital asymptomatic tooth.

Lower anterior sensitivity with inadequate keratinized tissue and high frenum: frenectomy plus free gingival graft is the classic way to increase KT and vestibule; CTG mainly adds thickness/coverage but FGG is preferred to gain KT band.
Gargiulo biologic width: junctional epithelium averages ~0.97 mm and connective tissue attachment ~1.07 mm (total ~2 mm supracrestal tissue). Values 0.4/0.7 mm do not match classic JE mean.
Active periodontitis requires a susceptible host plus pathogenic bacterial biofilm (dysbiosis). Fungi/viruses/autoimmunity are not the standard causal triad for chronic periodontitis.
Nabers (curved, color-coded) probe is designed to detect and classify furcation involvement. Williams/UNC are straight pocket probes; explorers detect calculus/caries, not grade furcations reliably.

Drug-induced enlargement from nifedipine: dentist must not stop the cardiac drug unilaterally—refer to the physician for possible substitution while providing OHI/SRP and, if needed, gingivectomy after medical coordination.

Among interdental osseous defects, craters (two-wall) are very common—commonly cited around one-third (~35%) of defects in classic periodontal literature—not 25/45/65 as a universal single figure without that context.
A pseudopocket (gingival pocket) is deepened sulcus from gingival enlargement/coronal soft-tissue excess without apical migration of attachment or true bone loss.
Diabetes and periodontitis have a bidirectional relationship: hyperglycemia worsens perio destruction/healing; untreated perio can impair glycemic control via systemic inflammation.
Periodontal surgery is indicated after cause-related therapy when residual deep pockets, furcation access needs, or regenerative anatomy remain—not before SRP and not for simple gingivitis.
Violating biologic width (~2 mm JE+CT above bone) with subgingival margins causes chronic inflammation, bone loss, and pocketing around the restoration.
Vertical/angular infrabony defects, especially 2–3 wall, may be candidates for regenerative therapy (GTR/biologics/grafts) when defect morphology and hygiene allow.
Nifedipine (calcium channel blocker) is classically associated with drug-influenced gingival enlargement along with phenytoin and cyclosporine.
Phenytoin is a classic anticonvulsant causing drug-influenced gingival enlargement; paracetamol and amoxicillin are not.

Radiolucency without reported pain fits asymptomatic apical periodontitis (AP with apical rarefaction, no clinical symptoms). Acute/symptomatic labels require pain/bite tenderness; “chronic periodontitis” is a perio diagnosis, not apical PA status.
Incisal edge display at smile should harmonize with the curvature of the lower lip (smile arc)—a key esthetic reference, not the upper lip alone.
Age 12 with molar–incisor bone loss, CAL ~3–4 mm (Stage II), and rapid destruction for age = molar-incisor pattern periodontitis Stage II Grade C (grade driven by %bone loss:age and youth).

Severe horizontal bone loss and mobility before/without proper ortho force application reflect severe periodontitis as the disease cause—not “heavy force” as the primary etiology of that bone loss pattern.
Second trimester is the preferred window; severe dental pain should be treated (with necessary radiographs and local anesthesia as indicated)—do not wait until after birth for acute odontogenic pain.

Vital #31 with grade III mobility, mild percussion tenderness, and implant proximity indicates primary periodontal destruction/trauma from the neighboring implant path—not primary endodontic disease (pulp tests vital).
Least true: plaque is not consistently higher in smokers than non-smokers. Smoking impairs host response and healing; microbial community shifts occur, but “more plaque” is the weak/false statement.

Severe lingering cold response (irreversible pulpitis/necrotic path) with isolated narrow deep pocket and no generalized bone loss = primary endodontic lesion with secondary periodontal breakdown along a sinus tract/pocket.

Most periodontally favorable finish line is slightly supragingival (~0.5 mm) when esthetics allow—facilitates hygiene and avoids biologic width violation; deep subgingival margins risk chronic inflammation.

Sickle scalers are for supragingival calculus (pointed tip, triangular cross-section, two cutting edges). Subgingival debridement uses curettes; sickles risk soft-tissue trauma if forced subgingivally.

Deep pocket on a previously treated tooth when endo-perio continuum is suspected: address endodontic failure/re-treatment first when pulpal origin likely, then periodontal therapy—sequence endo then perio for primary endo/secondary perio.
Fremitus grading: Grade 1 = slight palpable vibration only; Grade 2 = clearly palpable and slightly visible; Grade 3 = visible movement. Palpable but not visible = Grade 1.
Supernumerary teeth (mesiodens) result from disturbance in the initiation/bud stage of tooth development (extra tooth bud), not apposition of hard tissues.

Periodontal abscess on a vital tooth: drain (I&D and/or pocket irrigation/debridement), manage occlusion/plaque, and control systemic factors (diabetes). Antibiotics alone or immediate extraction are not first-line if restorable.

Desquamative gingivitis with white reticular lines on buccal mucosa = oral lichen planus. Pemphigus shows irregular ulcers/Nikolsky; EM is acute self-limited crusting; contact allergy lacks classic Wickham pattern.
Transgingival probing of ridge thickness under anesthesia to chart bone topography for implants is bone mapping (ridge mapping)—not periodontal pocket probing or augmentation itself.

Deep mesial pocket adjacent to a large mesial restoration: first remove overhangs and perform thorough deep scaling/root planing and reassess. Crown lengthening if biologic width violation after inflammation control; extraction not first for a single deep site.

Grade IV furcation (through-and-through with soft-tissue recession) has poor long-term retention prognosis; endo, graft, or regeneration alone rarely give predictable success—combined resective strategies or extraction are usually required.
Clinical attachment level equals probing depth when the gingival margin is at the CEJ (no recession, no coronal enlargement). CAL = PD + recession (or PD − coronal migration when GM is above CEJ).
Inflammation from margin to mucobuccal fold limited to few teeth = localized diffuse gingivitis. Marginal = free gingiva only; papillary = papillae only.
If the canine was luxated to rule out ankylosis yet erupts slowly, iatrogenic PDL/root injury during exposure/luxation can delay eruption more than residual bone alone when ankylosis was excluded.
Phase I always starts with cause control: scaling/root planing and hygiene for heavy plaque/inflammation before RPD or implants—prosthetics on inflamed foundations fail.

Lingually inclined lower anteriors in Kennedy Class I often need a labial bar (or swing-lock in selected cases) because a conventional lingual major connector contacts teeth poorly or traps plaque; lingual plate is for periodontally compromised with different indications.
Orthodontic tooth movement occurs via PDL pressure/tension remodeling (bone resorption/apposition). Without vital PDL (ankylosis), teeth do not move orthodontically.
≥5 teeth lost to perio + need for complex rehab → Stage IV; ≥10 cigarettes/day and rapid destruction → Grade C. PD 5–6 mm is compatible. Thus generalized Stage IV Grade C.

Child with progressive limited opening and deviation suggests TMJ ankylosis—definitive management is gap arthroplasty (± reconstruction), not exercises or splints alone once true bony ankylosis exists.
Gracey 11/12 is designed for mesial surfaces of posterior teeth. Gracey 13/14 is distal posterior; 1/2 is anterior; universals can work but 11/12 is the most appropriate listed area-specific choice.
Inflammation confined to interdental papillae of #11–13 = localized papillary gingivitis. Diffuse would involve attached gingiva/alveolar mucosa; marginal involves free gingival margins primarily.
Intraoral extraction with flap and no purulence is clean-contaminated (oral flora). Dirty = gross infection; clean = atraumatic sterile remote from aerodigestive tract (rare intraorally).

A restoration placed too far subgingivally that causes ongoing discomfort/BOP implicates biologic width violation and chronic inflammation. Slight overhangs also harm, but “too deep” points specifically to supracrestal attachment invasion.

Pre-radiotherapy, extract hopeless teeth with poor long-term prognosis (e.g., grade II mobility with perio compromise) to reduce osteoradionecrosis risk—more critical than isolated CAL 5 mm without mobility if restorable and cleanable.
Withholding food/fluids to hasten death is passive euthanasia (omission). Active euthanasia is deliberate lethal intervention; DNR is only about not resuscitating cardiopulmonary arrest.

Systemic antibiotics are not routine after SRP for all smokers; when adjuncts are chosen for severe anaerobic infection, metronidazole (often with amoxicillin) targets periopathogens. Doxy is more host-modulation/local delivery niche.
Nifedipine-related gingival enlargement: refer to prescribing physician for medically supervised drug substitution; dentist continues mechanical therapy—never stop antihypertensives independently.

Diabetic patient with periodontal/gingival abscess and deep wide pocket: drain/debride the abscess, manage perio infection and medical status, then restore caries—extraction only if hopeless; antibiotics alone insufficient.
Symphyseal mandibular fractures are typically fixed with two miniplates (tension band + inferior plate) for stability against torsional forces—not a single plate alone in standard AO principles.
Gingivitis biofilm is mixed (initially gram-positive facultatives, shifting toward gram-negative anaerobes as inflammation establishes)—not purely + or −.

Long-term exogenous steroids suppress HPA axis; for dental extraction, traditional exam teaching is to double the usual glucocorticoid dose on the day of surgery (stress dose) rather than ignore coverage—coordinate with physician for complex cases.
Measurement from CEJ to pocket base is clinical attachment loss (CAL). If that distance is 6 mm, CAL = 6 mm. Pocket depth alone is GM to base; pseudopocket lacks CAL from CEJ.

Multiple untreated caries, poor hygiene, labial frenum injury, and bruises on neck/arms in a child raise non-accidental injury and dental neglect—mandatory safeguarding pathway, not a primary perio diagnosis alone.
Cyclosporine is a classic immunosuppressant causing drug-influenced gingival enlargement (with phenytoin and CCBs). Amoxicillin and paracetamol are not.
Nifedipine (dihydropyridine CCB) is the medication most classically tested for drug-induced gingival enlargement among the listed options.
Tarnow: when contact point to crestal bone is ≤5 mm, complete papilla fill is highly predictable (~100%); at 6 mm ~56%; ≥7 mm fill is uncommon.
Stage I = early/mild CAL (1–2 mm) and bone loss; Grade A = slow progression. Not severe CAL, rapid Grade C destruction, or advanced mobility (higher stages).
The offset blade angle is a defining design feature of Gracey (area-specific) curettes, aligning the face correctly when the terminal shank is parallel to the root.

A partial-thickness (split) flap elevates epithelium and connective tissue while leaving periosteum on bone—used when periosteal blood supply must remain (e.g., free grafts). Full-thickness includes periosteum.
